tldr: Part V of a six-paper condensed-mathematics research program on topological phases of matter. It asks which classes in Part IV's abstract invertible condensed phase spectrum IP^cond_{d,G} are actually produced by explicit uniformly gapped lattice Hamiltonians, formalizing realizability as the essential surjectivity on pi_0 of a comparison map rho, and draws a sharp line between two nested notions: realizability by some gapped Hamiltonian versus by an exactly-solvable local commuting-projector model. Three theorems assembled from cited results anchor the positive and negative boundaries: every class in H^{d+1}(G,U(1)) is commuting-projector realizable via the Chen-Gu-Liu-Wen construction with Else-Nayak's edge-anomaly characterization (V-A); in spatial dimensions one and two, for on-site finite symmetry, Ogata's operator-algebraic index is a complete invariant, so realized equals invariant there (V-B); and the Kapustin-Fidkowski theorem shows commuting-projector Hamiltonians always have zero Hall conductance, so every chiral invertible class is excluded from the commuting-projector image (V-C). These are assembled into a dimension-by-symmetry status table, and three numbered conjectures address the open global picture, including a genuinely new condensed-cohomological descent criterion for realizability over profinite disorder hulls. Two fully proved elementary propositions -- the Kitaev-chain Z2 winding invariant and the cluster-state string order -- anchor the torsion corner concretely and are cross-checked by accompanying Haskell/QuickCheck code.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine physicists have a very abstract classification scheme -- built from bordism theory and algebraic topology -- that in principle lists every possible 'topological phase' a gapped quantum system could be in, somewhat like a periodic table lists every possible element. A natural question is whether every entry on that abstract list actually corresponds to something buildable: could you, in principle, write down an explicit quantum Hamiltonian -- a concrete rule for how a lattice of qubits or atoms interacts -- whose ground state realizes that abstract class? This paper, the fifth in a six-part mathematical series, is entirely about that question, which the authors call the 'realizability gap.'

The paper draws a sharp distinction between two different senses of 'can be built.' The weaker sense, 'gapped-realizable,' just means some Hamiltonian with an energy gap (protecting a stable ground state) produces the phase, with no constraint on how hard the Hamiltonian is to solve. The stronger sense, 'commuting-projector realizable,' means the Hamiltonian can be built from mutually compatible ('commuting') simple building blocks -- the exactly-solvable case that most textbook constructions use. Every commuting-projector model is automatically gapped-realizable, but the paper's central organizing fact -- a rigorous theorem due to Kapustin and Fidkowski -- is that the converse fails: any phase with a nonzero 'Hall conductance' (the effect behind the quantum Hall effect) or, more generally, a chiral edge can never be built from commuting projectors, even though such phases are unquestionably physically real and well understood by other methods. This is a hard mathematical wall, not an open engineering difficulty.

Beyond that wall, the paper surveys what is known to be buildable. Any topological phase protected by a finite symmetry group and classified by ordinary group cohomology can be built explicitly and exactly by a general recipe (Chen-Gu-Liu-Wen), and the simplest nontrivial example -- a chain of qubits called the cluster state -- is worked out by hand: a nonlocal 'string' measurement gives a perfect +1 in the protected phase and decays smoothly to zero as the protecting symmetry is broken, a claim the paper also verifies with an exact stabilizer-formalism computation. Similarly, the Kitaev chain, a toy model of a one-dimensional superconductor, hosts unpaired 'Majorana' excitations at its ends exactly when a simple integer-valued winding number is nonzero, also checked computationally across the full parameter plane. In dimensions one and two, for symmetries realized in the simplest ('on-site') way, an existing deep theorem (due to Ogata) shows that a measurable operator-algebraic index exactly matches the phase -- so in these special cases the realizability question is completely settled: everything the abstract classification predicts really can be built, and no two different exactly-solvable models secretly represent the same phase.

Having laid out what is proved, the paper turns to what remains open, framed as three explicit conjectures rather than claims: that the full collection of buildable phases is exactly the set of 'short-range-entangled' ones (a technical notion of having no exotic long-distance quantum correlations); that the phases blocked from commuting-projector construction (the chiral ones) can nonetheless always be built by some harder-to-solve gapped Hamiltonian; and, the paper's most original piece, that when a system has quenched disorder (impurities placed according to some fixed statistical rule, formalized here using a 'profinite' space, an idea borrowed from arithmetic), whether a phase survives that disorder is governed by a single algebraic obstruction that can in principle be checked resolution by resolution, rather than needing new hard analysis for every new kind of disorder.
key_contributions: Formalizes physical realizability as the essential surjectivity, on pi_0, of the comparison map rho: Phases_{d,G} -> C_{d,G} from the stabilized stack of lattice models to the abstract bordism/homotopy classification target of Part IV, with a complete proof that gapped-realizability of a class is equivalent to that class lying in the essential image of the comparison functor (Prop. 'esssurj').

States Theorem V-A: for every finite group G and every class in H^{d+1}(G,U(1)) there is an explicit G-symmetric local commuting-projector Hamiltonian realizing it, injective on the group-cohomology subgroup, with an anomalous boundary action exactly obstructed by that class -- assembled from the Chen-Gu-Liu-Wen fixed-point construction and the Else-Nayak edge characterization.

States Theorem V-B: in spatial dimension d=1 (H^2(G,U(1)) index) and d=2 (Ogata's H^3(G,T) index), for on-site finite symmetry, the operator-algebraic index is a complete invariant of the symmetric phase, so rho restricted to these SPT phases is a bijection onto the group-cohomology classification -- the only region of the paper where realizability is fully settled.

States Theorem V-C, the paper's hard obstruction: citing Kapustin-Fidkowski, any local commuting-projector Hamiltonian has vanishing Hall conductance (equivalently zero chiral central charge), so every invertible class with nonzero Hall response -- integer quantum Hall states, Chern insulators, the E8 state -- is gapped-realizable but never commuting-projector realizable (Cor. 'proper': im(rho^CP) is a proper subgroup of im(rho) whenever chiral phases exist).

Gives two fully self-contained elementary propositions with complete proofs -- the Kitaev-chain Z2 winding/Majorana-number invariant across the (mu,t) parameter plane (Prop. 'kitaev') and the cluster-state nonlocal string order together with its monotone degradation under a symmetry-breaking rotation (Prop. 'cluster') -- both cross-checked by an accompanying exact Haskell stabilizer-formalism engine and Bogoliubov-de Gennes winding-number computation with QuickCheck properties.

Assembles a dimension-by-symmetry status table separating the torsion part of the classification (settled or constructive via commuting-projector models: CGLW, Kitaev chain, Walker-Wang) from the free/chiral part (gapped-realizable but commuting-projector-obstructed: E8 state, Chern insulators), and states three numbered open conjectures on the global realizability image -- that it equals the short-range-entangled subspectrum (V-1), that chiral classes are always gapped-realizable though never by commuting projectors (V-2), and, the paper's most original proposal, that realizability over a profinite disorder hull F^{Z^d} is governed by a condensed-cohomological descent obstruction detectable at finite resolution (V-3).
audience: Mathematical physicists and operator algebraists working on the classification and explicit construction of symmetry-protected and topologically ordered phases (SPT classification, exactly-solvable/commuting-projector models, Hall response and chiral central charge); readers of the companion six-part series who need Part V's realizability results as the converse/completion of Part IV's abstract classification. The introduction, status table, and the Kitaev-chain and cluster-state worked examples are largely self-contained for anyone with graduate-level background in topological phases of matter, though full appreciation of the profinite-disorder conjecture (V-3) benefits from familiarity with condensed mathematics (Clausen-Scholze).
